principle.name: SolidJS-First Architecture
principle.description: Always implement fine-grained reactivity patterns inspired by SolidJS. Avoid VDOM reconciliation approaches at all costs.
principle.rationale: SolidJS's fine-grained reactivity eliminates unnecessary computations and DOM updates, providing superior performance and predictable behavior compared to VDOM-based frameworks.

example.good: Use signals that directly trigger specific DOM updates
example.bad: Implement component re-rendering with virtual DOM diffing

principle.name: Performance-First Design
principle.description: Every design decision must prioritize runtime performance and memory efficiency. Profile early and often, especially for WASM compilation.
principle.rationale: Go's performance advantages can be negated by poor architecture choices. WASM introduces additional constraints that require careful consideration.

example.good: Batch DOM updates and minimize allocations
example.bad: Trigger immediate DOM updates for each signal change

principle.name: Idiomatic Go Patterns
principle.description: Translate JavaScript reactive patterns to idiomatic Go code that feels natural to Go developers while maintaining reactive principles.
principle.rationale: The framework should feel like Go, not like JavaScript translated to Go. This improves adoption and maintainability.

example.good: Use channels and goroutines for async effects

convention.rule: Signals should be minimal and focused on single values
Effects should be pure and deterministic where possible
Use direct DOM manipulation, never virtual DOM

techniques.technique: Use object pooling for frequently created/destroyed objects
Prefer value types over pointers where appropriate
Cache DOM elements and reuse them
Batch DOM operations to minimize syscall/js overhead

guideline.rule: Optimize for WASM compilation and runtime

techniques.technique: Use efficient data structures for WASM environment
Avoid deep call stacks that can impact WASM performance
Profile WASM binary size and optimize imports

guideline.rule: Optimize signal graph traversal and updates

techniques.technique: Use topological ordering for effect execution
Batch related updates to minimize DOM thrashing
Use weak references where appropriate to prevent memory leaks

pitfall.why_problematic: Defeats the performance advantages of fine-grained reactivity and introduces unnecessary complexity and computational overhead
pitfall.correct_approach: Design direct DOM bindings that update only when specific signals change, following SolidJS's compilation model

pitfall.why_problematic: Reactive systems can trigger frequently, making memory allocation patterns critical for performance
pitfall.correct_approach: Use pre-allocated buffers, object pools, and minimize garbage collection pressure in signal/effect execution paths

pitfall.why_problematic: Each DOM update requires expensive syscall/js calls and can cause layout thrashing
pitfall.correct_approach: Implement update batching and scheduling to minimize DOM manipulation frequency while maintaining reactive semantics

category.name: before_implementation
category.item: Understand the SolidJS pattern being translated
Design for zero unnecessary DOM updates

category.name: before_completion
category.item: Profile memory usage and allocation patterns
Verify no VDOM-like patterns were introduced
Validate WASM binary size and startup performance

solidjs_advantages_to_maintain.advantage: Direct DOM manipulation without abstraction layers
